content:
While a minion with the patrol keyword is engaged with a player, that player cannot use cards they control to thwart the main scheme.

  • Equivalent to:
    The engaged player cannot thwart the main scheme.
See: Cost.
The per-player icon next to a value multiplies that value by the number of players who started the scenario. If a player is eliminated, this value does not change.

While a player is resolving a card with the peril keyword, that player cannot consult other players, and other players cannot play cards or trigger abilities. Equivalent to: 'While a player is resolving this card, that player cannot consult other players, and other players cannot trigger abilities.

While this card is in a player's play area, other players cannot trigger abilities on this card.'

• A card with the permanent keyword cannot be defeated, leave play, or have any part of its text box blanked, except by card abilities in the same set (hero set, scenario set, or modular set). Equivalent to: 'Set this card aside during setup.
  • Effects on cards not from this card's set cannot defeat this card, remove this card from play, or blank any part of its text box.' Permanent cards are set aside before step 1 of setup and put into play later. They do not count toward deck size, and are not valid targets for effects causing them to leave play (such effects instead target the non-permanent card fitting the criteria).
  • If a card to which a permanent attachment was attached leaves play, resolve its 'attach to' text (or remove it from the game).
  • If a player is eliminated while a non-attachment permanent card they don't own is in their play area, remove that card from the game.
A physical resource is one of the four types of resources. Physical resources can be spent to pay resource costs; some abilities specifically require them.

An attack with the piercing keyword discards any tough status cards from the attacked character before dealing damage.

  • Equivalent to:
    Before this attack deals damage to a character, discard each tough status card from that character.
  • If an attack with piercing would deal no damage, it does not discard tough status cards.

Two types of play areas: a player's play area and the villain's play area.

  • A player's play area contains identity, deck, hand, discard pile, cards in play under their control, and any facedown/faceup encounter cards in their area.
  • The villain's play area contains the villain deck, main scheme deck, encounter deck, encounter discard pile, and encounter cards not in a player's play area.
  • A card cannot be in more than one play area at a time.

Playing a card involves paying its cost and placing it in the play area, causing it to enter play (or, for an event, resolve its ability and be placed in the discard pile).

  • Cards are played from a player's hand. Abilities may cause cards to be put into play, bypassing the cost and any playing restrictions; a card put into play enters play in its controller's play area.
  • Cards with '[type] form only' can only be played/put into play by a player in that form.
  • When an event is played, place it on the table, resolve its ability, then discard it.
  • A card put into play is not considered to have been played; when put into play, its resource cost is ignored.

Many cards and abilities contain specific instructions pertaining to when/how they may or may not be used, or conditions that must be true to use them. In order to use an ability or play a card, all of its play restrictions must be observed.

A permission is an optional play restriction allowing a player to play a card/use an ability outside normal timing/specifications (e.g. playing an ally from the discard pile).

• A player is a person playing Marvel Champions.
  • Each player has their own personal play area containing identity card, hit point dial, hand, deck, discard pile, allies/upgrades/supports in play, and attachments/minions/obligations in their area.
  • During each player phase, each player takes their own turn.
  • Each player owns the cards contained within their deck (including identity card) at the start of the game.
  • The 'first player' is the player who has the first player token.

There are seven player card types: ally, event, identity, player side scheme, resource, support, and upgrade. Player cards may belong to various classifications (identity-specific, aspect).

Most player cards have a blue card back.

• A player's deck contains the player cards owned by the player who started the game with that deck. Order cannot be changed unless instructed.
  • If a player deck empties, the player shuffles their discard pile to make a new deck and immediately deals themselves one facedown encounter card from the top of the encounter deck.
  • If the deck empties and reshuffles while drawing, the player continues drawing up to the specified number; if while discarding, no further cards are discarded from the newly shuffled deck.
  • If it empties and there are no cards in the discard pile, it does not reset until there is at least one card in the discard pile.
See: Discard Pile.

A player is eliminated if their identity is defeated (usually reduced to zero remaining hit points). When eliminated, in order:

  1. Pass the first player token if held.
  2. Minions engaged with the eliminated player engage the next clockwise player (retaining tokens, attached cards, boost cards, tucked cards, status cards).
  3. For each card in their play area not owned by them: permanent attachments resolve 'attach to' text or are removed from the game; non-attachment permanent cards are removed from the game; other cards go to owner's discard pile.
  4. Place each card owned by the eliminated player in their own discard pile.
  5. Remove the eliminated player's play area and all game elements within it from the game. Remaining players continue playing; eliminated players win or lose with the group depending on how they finish. If all players are eliminated, the game ends and the players lose. If eliminated partway through an ability's resolution, resolve the entire ability (an attack in progress ends). Effects referring to the players in the game ignore eliminated players, except for the per-player icon.

During the player phase, each player (in player order) takes one turn. After each player has taken a turn, the players discard down or draw up to their hand size and ready each exhausted card.

Effects lasting 'until the end of the player phase' end after players draw up and ready; then effects resolving 'when/after the player phase ends' are resolved.

Player side schemes are missions heroes undertake to facilitate defeating the villain — the player card equivalent of side schemes in the encounter deck.

  • When one enters play, it's placed next to the main scheme in the villain's play area, with threat equal to its starting threat value.
  • The number in play is limited by the player side scheme limit. It remains in play until there's no threat on it (defeated, discarded or, with victory X, added to the victory display) or discarded due to exceeding the limit. Rules/effects referring to 'schemes' or 'side schemes' also refer to player side schemes.

The number of player side schemes in play at any time is limited: one if one/two players started the game, two if three/four players started. If there are ever more than the limit, the first player chooses and discards until at the limit.

A player may play a player side scheme even at the limit, but must then choose one to discard (not considered defeated).

During their turn, a player may perform the following options in any order (each performable as many times as able except 'change form,' once per turn): Change form; Play an ally/upgrade/support/player side scheme from hand; Use alter-ego's basic recovery (alter-ego form) or hero's basic attack/thwart (hero form); Use an ally in play to attack/thwart; Trigger an 'Action' ability on a controlled card in play, an encounter card in play, any card with text allowing it, or an event card in hand (by playing it); Ask another player to trigger any 'Action' ability that player could trigger.

• A player's play area (also 'player's game area') is where a player's identity card, deck, hand, discard pile, and hit point dial are located. Ally, support, and upgrade cards are placed here when entering play. Attachment cards attached to cards in the villain's play area are not in the player's play area. Minion cards engaged with a player are in that player's play area, not the villain's. Obligation cards given to a player are in that player's play area.
  • If a player is eliminated, their play area is removed from the game.
See: Initiating Abilities.

• Some card abilities prevent damage or threat.
  • When damage is prevented, reduce the amount the target takes (not the amount dealt).
  • If an effect prevents all damage, the character is not considered to have taken damage.
  • If all damage from an attack is prevented, the attacker is considered to have dealt damage but not to have 'attacked and damaged' the target.
  • If dealing damage is a cost, that cost is considered paid even if prevented; if taking damage is a cost, it is not considered paid unless all was taken.
  • When threat is prevented, reduce the amount assigned before it is placed on the scheme.

The word 'printed' refers to the text, characteristic, or value physically printed on the card. If a player must pay a cost using 'printed resources,' they may use resources generated by a card's ability if the icon(s) generated is printed in its text box.

Wild resources cannot be spent as other resource types for such a cost.
